common_name: Red Sister Ti Plant (Brilliant Red-Foliage Cordyline Tropical Accent)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to bright indirect light; protect from direct afternoon sun in summer which can bleach and burn the red leaf pigment in the intense Gulf South summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to bright indirect light; Red Sister is the most widely planted ti plant (Cordyline fruticosa) cultivar in Gulf South landscaping — producing long, strap-shaped, arching leaves in brilliant burgundy-red to rose-red and magenta-pink tones that provide a year-round tropical foliage focal point in warm-climate gardens. The name 'ti plant' refers to the Pacific Islander cultural significance of Cordyline fruticosa, where the plant carries deep spiritual and traditional meaning and is widely used in ceremonial contexts throughout Polynesia and Hawaii. 'Red Sister' is one of the most intensely and uniformly red ti plant cultivars — the long, lance-shaped leaves (12–24 inches) are a rich, saturated red-magenta through much of the year, sometimes with lighter pink margins that create a luminous, two-tone effect. In zone 9b-12, Red Sister grows as a perennial tropical shrub reaching 3–6 feet with arching rosettes of foliage; in zone 9a, it is often used as a container plant moved indoors before frost or as a warm-season annual-style tropical replaced each spring. The bold red foliage creates outstanding contrast with green-foliaged tropical plants — ginger, elephant ear, bird of paradise — where the red provides the warm color accent that defines a classic tropical border composition.

common_name: Dwarf Yaupon Holly (Native Compact Evergreen Shrub for Gulf South Landscapes)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Yaupon is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for Gulf South landscapes — tolerating clay soils, sandy soils, salt spray, poor drainage, and drought with equal reliability.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Yaupon Holly ('Nana') is among the most widely planted native shrubs in Gulf South commercial and residential landscapes — a compact, rounded, densely branched form of the native yaupon holly (Ilex vomitoria) that reaches only 24–36 inches in height while spreading slightly wider, creating a naturally mounded, low-maintenance shape that requires minimal pruning to maintain a tidy appearance. The small, oval, dark green, finely toothed leaves are attractive year-round; 'Nana' is typically male and does not produce the profuse bright red berry clusters of the female species, though the evergreen foliage provides consistent ornamental value and wildlife habitat. Yaupon holly is the only native North American plant known to contain caffeine — the species was consumed as a ceremonial beverage by many Native American groups throughout the Southeast. The extraordinary adaptability of Dwarf Yaupon makes it one of the few plants reliable across the full range of Gulf South conditions: heavy clay soils, sandy coastal soils, periodic flooding, extended drought, salt spray tolerance for coastal plantings, and the range of pH conditions across the region. The native origin means it supports native insect communities and requires no pesticides when grown in appropriate conditions. Outstanding as a low hedge, mass planting on slopes, foundation shrub, and coastal landscape plant.

common_name: Texas Columbine (Native Longspurred Yellow Wildflower Columbine)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Aquilegia longissima requires cold stratification for reliable germination — fall sowing outdoors provides natural stratification; seeds benefit from surface sowing as light aids germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade with afternoon protection from the hottest sun; Texas Columbine (Aquilegia longissima) is a native wildflower of the Edwards Plateau and the Chihuahuan Desert transitional zone, producing pale golden-yellow to cream-yellow flowers with extraordinarily long, elegant spurs reaching 4–6 inches — the longest flower spurs of any North American native columbine, creating a distinctly different and more delicate floral form than the compact-spurred garden columbine hybrids. The very long, thread-like spurs are specifically adapted to pollination by hawk moths (Sphingidae) whose long proboscises can reach the nectar at the spur tip — a co-evolutionary relationship that has driven the extreme spur length unique to this species. Aquilegia longissima is more heat-tolerant than most columbine species, performing reliably in zone 9 where other columbines are short-lived or summer dormant — making it one of the few columbines that works in Gulf South gardens as a reliable perennial rather than a brief cool-season performer. The airy, finely textured blue-green foliage is attractive from fall through spring, and the elegant flowers in April–June attract hummingbirds and hawk moths. Outstanding naturalized under deciduous trees and along shaded woodland garden edges where it self-sows modestly to maintain its population.

common_name: Emerald Giant Sweet Pepper (Very Large Thick-Walled Green Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost; Emerald Giant is a commercial-type pepper that responds well to consistent fertility — apply a balanced fertilizer at transplanting and a calcium-containing fertilizer mid-season to prevent blossom-end rot.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost date; Emerald Giant is one of the standard large-fruited bell pepper varieties for American home and commercial gardens — producing very large (4–5 inch long, 3–4 inch wide), blocky, 4-lobed, thick-walled green bell peppers with the classic sweet bell pepper flavor and firm, crunchy flesh that defines the standard bell pepper for American cooking. The name reflects both the large fruit size ('Giant') and the deep green color at the typical fresh-market harvest stage — the brilliant, deep-green, shiny-skinned fruits are the archetypal green bell pepper form. Left to ripen fully, Emerald Giant turns red, producing a sweeter, more complex flavor than the green stage. The thick walls (3/8–1/2 inch) provide more usable pepper flesh per fruit than thin-walled types, better heat retention in cooking preparations, and superior durability at harvest. In zone 9, bell peppers produce best in the spring season (March–June) and a brief fall window (September–October) — the extended summer heat reduces fruit set and causes sunscald on exposed fruits. The large, heavy fruits on 'Emerald Giant' require staking or caging to prevent branch breakage when the plant is heavily loaded.

common_name: Aimee Gardenia (Very Large Flower Exhibition Gardenia from Louisiana)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 5.0–6.0) soil in partial shade; 'Aimee Yoshiba' produces some of the largest gardenia flowers available — flowers can reach 4–5 inches across, making individual blooms among the most spectacular of any gardenia.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 5.0–6.0) soil in partial shade to morning sun with afternoon shade protection; Aimee (also known as 'Aimee Yoshiba') is a Louisiana-associated gardenia cultivar producing some of the largest flowers of any commonly grown Gardenia jasminoides — individual blooms reaching 4–5 inches across with many rows of creamy white, wavy, overlapping petals that create a full, formal double flower of extraordinary refinement. The flowers open creamy white and age to a warm ivory-yellow, with the same intense, classic gardenia fragrance that has made gardenias the most beloved fragrant landscape shrub in the American South. 'Aimee' grows as a vigorous, upright-spreading shrub to 5–7 feet in typical Gulf South gardens, larger and more open in habit than compact cultivars like 'Radicans' or 'Chuck Hayes'. The spring bloom (April–June) is the primary season, often with sporadic rebloom in fall if conditions are favorable. As with all Gardenia jasminoides, 'Aimee' requires strongly acidic soil for healthy growth — iron chlorosis (yellowing leaves with green veins) is the primary symptom of insufficient acidity, corrected with sulfur soil acidification and acid-forming fertilizers. The large flower size and outstanding fragrance make 'Aimee' the preferred gardenia for cut flower use, where a single bloom can fragrance a room for several days.
